label: Berlin — Planck's Quantum Hypothesis
sub: On December 14, 1900, Max Planck presented his blackbody radiation formula to the German Physical Society (DPG) in Berlin, introducing the quantum of energy E=hν and founding quantum theory.

label: Copenhagen — Niels Bohr Institute
sub: Founded by Niels Bohr in 1920, the Niels Bohr Institute in Copenhagen became the world's leading center for quantum physics in the 1920s–30s, hosting Heisenberg, Dirac, Pauli, Gamow, and many others who developed the Copenhagen interpretation.

label: Göttingen — Matrix Mechanics
sub: Max Born's Göttingen group, including Born, Jordan, and the young Heisenberg, formalized quantum mechanics as matrix mechanics in 1925, establishing the canonical commutation relations and mathematical framework of quantum theory.

label: Helgoland — Heisenberg's Breakthrough
sub: Working alone on the North Sea island of Helgoland in June 1925 to escape hay fever, Werner Heisenberg had the key insight that quantum mechanics should be based only on observable quantities, leading directly to matrix mechanics.

label: Cambridge — Cavendish Laboratory
sub: The Cavendish Laboratory at Cambridge was the site of Rutherford's nuclear model (1911), Chadwick's neutron discovery (1932), and the early development of particle physics. Paul Dirac developed his relativistic quantum theory here.

label: Zürich — Schrödinger's Wave Equation
sub: Erwin Schrödinger developed his wave equation while Professor at the University of Zürich in early 1926, submitting the first paper on January 27, 1926. ETH Zürich and the University of Zürich have remained major quantum physics centers.

label: Brussels — Solvay Conferences
sub: Brussels hosted the landmark Solvay Conferences from 1911 onward. The Fifth Solvay Conference (1927) on 'Electrons and Photons' produced the famous group photograph of quantum theory founders and was the arena for the Bohr-Einstein debates.

label: Paris — De Broglie's Matter Waves
sub: Louis de Broglie submitted his doctoral thesis at the University of Paris (Sorbonne) in 1924, proposing that all matter has an associated wavelength λ=h/p. Einstein endorsed the idea; experimental confirmation came in 1927.

label: Munich — Sommerfeld's School
sub: Arnold Sommerfeld's theoretical physics group at the Ludwig Maximilian University in Munich trained an extraordinary generation of quantum physicists, including Heisenberg, Pauli, Debye, and Bethe. The Munich school extended Bohr's model with elliptical orbits and relativity.

label: Frankfurt — Stern-Gerlach Experiment
sub: In 1922, Otto Stern and Walther Gerlach performed their famous experiment at the University of Frankfurt, passing silver atoms through an inhomogeneous magnetic field and observing discrete spatial quantization — direct evidence for angular momentum quantization.

label: Princeton — IAS (Einstein's Office)
sub: Albert Einstein spent the last 22 years of his life (1933–1955) at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, continuing his search for a unified field theory and his lifelong debate with Bohr over quantum foundations, including the EPR paper (co-written nearby in 1935).

label: Los Alamos — Manhattan Project
sub: Los Alamos National Laboratory, founded in 1943 under J. Robert Oppenheimer, was the center of the Manhattan Project, applying quantum mechanical understanding of nuclear fission to develop the first atomic bombs. Feynman, Fermi, Bethe, Teller, and many others worked here.

label: Geneva — CERN
sub: CERN (European Centre for Nuclear Research), established in 1954 on the Franco-Swiss border near Geneva, has been home to landmark quantum field theory experiments, Bell's theorem work (Bell was a CERN physicist), and the 2012 Higgs boson discovery at the LHC.

label: Oxford — Deutsch's Quantum Computing Theory
sub: David Deutsch at Oxford University published the foundational quantum computing paper in 1985, introducing the quantum circuit model and the first quantum algorithm. Oxford's quantum group remains a world leader in theoretical and experimental quantum information science.

label: Orsay — Aspect's Bell Test Experiments
sub: Alain Aspect performed his landmark Bell inequality experiments at the Institut d'Optique in Orsay, France in 1981–1982, closing the locality loophole for the first time using time-varying polarizers. These experiments earned Aspect the 2022 Nobel Prize.

label: Delft — Loophole-Free Bell Test (2015)
sub: Hensen et al. at Delft University of Technology achieved the first loophole-free Bell test in October 2015, using electron spins in nitrogen-vacancy centers separated by 1.3 km. Nature named this experiment one of the breakthroughs of 2015.

label: Murray Hill — Bell Laboratories
sub: Bell Labs in Murray Hill, NJ was the birthplace of the transistor (1947), the Davisson-Germer electron diffraction experiment (1927), Shor's and Grover's quantum algorithms (1994–1996), and a major center for quantum information theory throughout the 20th century.

label: Cambridge MA — MIT Quantum Research
sub: MIT hosted Feynman's landmark 1981 conference proposal for quantum computers and remains a major center of quantum computing research, including the MIT Quantum Photonics group and collaborations with IBM and Google on quantum hardware.

label: Pasadena — Caltech (Feynman)
sub: Richard Feynman was professor at Caltech from 1950 until his death in 1988, where he developed his path integral formulation of quantum mechanics, co-developed QED, formulated Feynman diagrams, and proposed quantum computing. The Feynman Lectures on Physics were delivered here.

label: Innsbruck — First Quantum Teleportation
sub: Anton Zeilinger's group at the University of Innsbruck performed the first experimental quantum teleportation in 1997, transferring the quantum state of a photon using entanglement and classical communication. The paper in Nature marked the launch of experimental quantum information science in Europe.

label: Stockholm — Nobel Prize Ceremonies
sub: Stockholm's Concert Hall has been the site of Nobel Prize award ceremonies since 1926, where quantum physicists from Planck and Einstein through Heisenberg, Schrödinger, Dirac, and most recently Aspect, Clauser, and Zeilinger (2022) have received recognition for fundamental contributions to quantum theory.

label: Bern — Einstein's Patent Office
sub: Albert Einstein wrote his four landmark 1905 papers — including the photoelectric effect paper founding quantum theory — while working as a patent clerk at the Swiss Patent Office in Bern. The 'miracle year' papers were written in his spare time without access to a university lab.

label: Leiden — Superconductivity Discovery
sub: Heike Kamerlingh Onnes at Leiden University discovered superconductivity in mercury at 4.2 K in 1911 — a quantum phenomenon that required 46 years of theoretical effort (BCS theory, 1957) to explain. Leiden was also a major center of quantum statistical mechanics through Lorentz and Ehrenfest.

label: Yorktown Heights — IBM Quantum Research
sub: IBM's Thomas J. Watson Research Center in Yorktown Heights has been a hub of quantum computing development since the 1990s, leading to the IBM Quantum Experience (2016), the 127-qubit Eagle (2021), the 433-qubit Osprey (2022), and the 1,121-qubit Condor (2023) processors.

label: Mountain View — Google Quantum AI
sub: Google's quantum AI campus in Mountain View developed the Sycamore 53-qubit processor that claimed quantum supremacy in October 2019, the first processor to demonstrate a computational task that would take classical supercomputers thousands of years.

label: Burnaby — D-Wave Systems
sub: D-Wave Systems, headquartered in Burnaby, British Columbia, built and sold the world's first commercial quantum computer (D-Wave One, 2011) based on quantum annealing, with clients including Lockheed Martin, Google, and NASA. Its approach differs fundamentally from gate-based quantum computing.

label: Waterloo — Perimeter Institute
sub: The Perimeter Institute for Theoretical Physics in Waterloo, Ontario (founded 1999) has become a world-leading research center for quantum foundations, quantum information, and quantum gravity, hosting researchers working on quantum computing theory, interpretations, and quantum error correction.

label: Vienna — Schrödinger & Zeilinger
sub: Vienna was the birthplace of Erwin Schrödinger (1887) and home to Anton Zeilinger's pioneering quantum optics and entanglement research at the University of Vienna, including long-distance quantum teleportation records. Vienna is a major European quantum technology hub.

label: Bristol — Dirac's Birthplace
sub: Paul Adrien Maurice Dirac was born in Bristol in 1902 and trained as an engineer before turning to theoretical physics. His derivation of the relativistic quantum equation bearing his name and his prediction of antimatter are among the greatest achievements in 20th-century physics.

label: Tokyo — Tomonaga's QED (Tokyo University)
sub: Sin-Itiro Tomonaga developed his independent formulation of renormalized quantum electrodynamics at Tokyo University during and after World War II, under extremely difficult conditions with limited access to foreign journals. His work independently paralleled Schwinger and Feynman, earning a shared 1965 Nobel Prize.

label: New York — Columbia University (Lamb Shift)
sub: Willis Lamb and Robert Retherford measured the Lamb shift in hydrogen at Columbia University in 1947 using microwave spectroscopy, detecting a tiny splitting between energy levels that Dirac's equation predicted to be identical. This precision measurement drove the development of renormalized QED.

label: Boulder — JILA (Bose-Einstein Condensate)
sub: Eric Cornell and Carl Wieman at JILA (Joint Institute for Laboratory Astrophysics) at CU Boulder created the first true Bose-Einstein Condensate in rubidium-87 at 170 nanokelvin in June 1995, confirming a 70-year-old prediction. Cornell and Wieman shared the 2001 Nobel Prize with Wolfgang Ketterle (MIT).

label: Wrocław — Max Born's Birthplace
sub: Max Born was born in Breslau (now Wrocław, Poland) in 1882. His probabilistic interpretation of the wave function (|ψ|² = probability density), proposed in 1926, is arguably the most fundamental conceptual contribution to quantum theory — establishing indeterminism as intrinsic to quantum mechanics.

label: Chicago — First Nuclear Reactor (CP-1)
sub: On December 2, 1942, Enrico Fermi's team at the University of Chicago's Stagg Field achieved the first controlled nuclear chain reaction in Chicago Pile-1 — applying quantum mechanical understanding of neutron-induced fission to create a sustained chain reaction, demonstrating the feasibility of nuclear power.

label: Barcelona — ICFO Quantum Optics
sub: ICFO (Institut de Ciències Fotòniques) in the Barcelona area is a leading European center for quantum optics, quantum communication, and quantum photonics research, contributing to entanglement-based quantum networks and single-photon quantum technologies.

label: Cambridge — Quantum Computing Centre (UK)
sub: Cambridge Quantum Computing (CQC), founded near Cambridge in 2014 and merged with Honeywell Quantum Solutions to form Quantinuum in 2021, developed the TKET quantum compiler and operates trapped-ion quantum hardware. Cambridge also hosts the Cavendish quantum devices group.

label: San Francisco Bay Area — IonQ (Trapped Ion)
sub: IonQ, headquartered in the Bay Area (originally College Park, MD), develops trapped-ion quantum computers claiming high-fidelity gate operations. IonQ went public in 2021 and claims the highest quantum volume among public cloud quantum hardware.

label: Munich — IBM Quantum Europe Hub
sub: IBM's Quantum Network hub in Munich at the Fraunhofer Institute / Technical University Munich gives European researchers access to IBM quantum processors via the cloud, supporting Germany's €2 billion quantum computing investment announced in 2021.

label: Hefei — USTC Quantum Info Lab (Jian-Wei Pan)
sub: Jian-Wei Pan's group at the University of Science and Technology of China (USTC) in Hefei leads China's quantum information program. They demonstrated quantum satellite-based entanglement distribution (Micius, 2017), quantum supremacy with photons (Jiuzhang, 2020), and boast the world's most extensive quantum communication network.

label: Toronto — Xanadu Photonic Quantum Computing
sub: Xanadu, headquartered in Toronto, develops photonic quantum computers using continuous-variable quantum optics and the PennyLane open-source quantum programming framework. Their Borealis photonic processor demonstrated quantum computational advantage in Gaussian boson sampling in 2022.
